macro_segments.title: Woman's Lack of Chazaka in Husband's Property and the Presumption of Non-Objection

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishna: A Woman Does Not Have Chazaka in Her Husband's Property
The Gemara's Explanation: Presumption Based on Husband's Maintenance Obligations
The Case Where the Husband Designated a Different Field for Her Support

macro_segments.title: Validity of Sale Transactions Between Husband and Wife

micro_segments.title: Inference from the Mishna: If Chazaka Doesn't Apply, Does a Document (Shtar) Suffice?
The Gemara's Doubt: Could the Sale Be Merely a Ruse to Uncover Hidden Funds?
Rav Nachman's Reported Position: Sale to Wife Is Valid and Not Merely to Discover Funds
Tosafot's Explanation: The Order of Money and Shtar Matters for Validity

macro_segments.title: The Debate Over Whether Shtar Mechira (Document of Sale) Requires Money to Effect Transfer

micro_segments.title: Shmuel's Position: Shtar Mechira Does Not Work Without Money Changing Hands
Rav Hamnuna's Challenge: The Mishna Shows Shtar Works Even Without Money
Rav Hamnuna's Resolution: The Mishna Applies Only When Selling Defective Property
Rav Ashi's Alternative: Interpreting the Mishna's Language as a Gift Despite Saying 'Sale'
Tosafot's Possibility: Using Multiple Shtarot for Different Legal Purposes

macro_segments.title: Application to Husband-Wife Transactions and the Distinction from Borrowing

micro_segments.title: The Baraita: Borrowed Money and Freed Slaves—Debts Are Not Collectible
The Gemara's Reasoning: The Debtor Claims He Was Merely Accessing His Own Funds
The Distinction: Why Sale Differs from Loan in the Context of Husband-Wife Relations

macro_segments.title: The Three Positions on Husband Selling Land to Wife

micro_segments.title: Rav Huna bar Avin's Report: Sale to Wife Is Valid and Husband Eats the Fruits
The Minority View: Even as Sale, It Should Be Treated as a Gift (Rav's Grandsons vs. Rav Chisda)
Rav's Final Position: Sale Means Sale; Gift Means Gift
Rabbi Eliezer's Position: Both Sale and Gift Result in Wife's Full Ownership

macro_segments.title: Rava's Distinction Based on Source of Funds: Hidden vs. Non-Hidden Money

micro_segments.title: Rava's Opening: Sale to Wife Is Invalid—Husband Only Seeking Access to Funds
The Gemara's Apparent Contradiction: Rava Says Both Invalid and Valid
Resolution: Hidden Funds Make the Sale Invalid; Non-Hidden Funds Make It Valid
Rav Yehuda's Practical Rule: Evidence of Hidden Account Determines Invalidity

macro_segments.title: Third-Party Depositaries: Accepting and Returning Deposits from Women, Slaves, and Minors

micro_segments.title: The Baraita: General Prohibition on Accepting Deposits from Women, Slaves, and Minors
Wife's Deposit: Return to Wife Unless She Dies, Then to Husband
Slave's Deposit: Return to Slave or to Master If Slave Dies
Minor's Deposit: Invest for Protection, Return When of Age or to Heirs If Dies
Deathbed Declaration: Recipient May Act on Deathbed Instruction to Give to Third Party

macro_segments.title: The Case of Rabba Bar Bar Channah's Wife: Deathbed Claims and Spousal Trust

micro_segments.title: The Case: Wife on Deathbed Claims Jewelry Belongs to Marta, Not Husband
Rav's Ruling: If Husband Trusts Wife's Honesty, Follow Her Instructions
Alternative: If Husband Distrusts Wife, Reinterpret Her Words to Preserve His Rights
The Alternate Opinion: If the Claim Is Reasonable and Makes Sense, Follow It

macro_segments.title: Synthesis: The Underlying Suspicion of Hidden Marital Funds Throughout the Sugia

micro_segments.title: The Common Thread: Husband's Potential Rights to Wife's Hidden or Unreported Funds
Case-by-Case Application: When Do We Believe the Husband's Claim vs. the Wife's Claim?
Open Question: Tosafot's Speculation on Husband Forcibly Taking Wife's Money
